QuestionText: The "golden hour" in trauma refers to:
Choice1: The time from injury to definitive surgical care
Choice2: The first hour of your shift before the tones drop
Choice3: How long it takes the ER to acknowledge you're standing there
Choice4: The window between coffee and the first call
Feedback: The golden hour concept (Cowley, 1975) holds that surgical intervention within 60 minutes of injury significantly improves survival in major trauma. Time matters — transport decisions should reflect it.

QuestionText: Scene safety should be continuously re-evaluated throughout the call, not just on arrival.
Feedback: Scene dynamics change. The calm front porch can become a problem at any point. Continuous awareness isn't paranoia — it's professional.

QuestionText: The correct compression-to-ventilation ratio for single-rescuer adult CPR is:
Choice2: 15:2, because that's what you learned in 1987 and you're sticking with it
Choice3: As many as possible before your back gives out
Choice4: Whatever the bystander was doing when you arrived
Feedback: 30:2 per AHA guidelines. Minimize interruptions — chest compressions drive perfusion pressure. Every pause costs you.

QuestionText: A patient presents with JVD, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds on one side, and hypotension. You suspect:
Choice3: Something the ED will sort out
Choice4: A very dramatic presentation of anxiety
Feedback: Classic tension pneumo presentation. Needle decompression is the field fix — second intercostal space, midclavicular line. Don't wait for confirmation; treat the clinical picture.

QuestionText: The PRIMARY treatment for a patient in anaphylactic shock is:
Choice4: Ask what they ate — thoroughly and at length
Feedback: Epinephrine (0.3–0.5mg IM, lateral thigh) is first-line. It reverses bronchospasm and vasodilation simultaneously. Antihistamines are adjuncts — they don't stop the anaphylaxis cascade.

QuestionText: A patient who says "I'm fine" immediately after a high-speed MVC still requires a thorough assessment.
Feedback: Adrenaline suppresses pain perception. Mechanism of injury drives your assessment — a 50mph delta-V doesn't care how the patient feels in the moment.

QuestionText: The MOST appropriate initial approach to a combative patient:
Choice1: Ensure scene safety, request law enforcement if needed, attempt verbal de-escalation
Choice3: Document "patient uncooperative," back slowly toward the exit
Choice4: Offer a snack — low blood sugar explains everything
Feedback: Safety first, always. Verbal de-escalation buys time and information. If law enforcement is needed, involve them early — waiting until things escalate is too late.

QuestionText: A responsive adult patient may legally refuse treatment and transport if:
Choice1: They are alert, oriented, understand the risks, and are not impaired
Choice2: They look like they mean it
Choice3: They pinky promise to see a doctor tomorrow
Choice4: The receiving hospital is on divert anyway
Feedback: Informed refusal requires decision-making capacity: alert, oriented, understands risks and alternatives, and not impaired. Document thoroughly — refusal calls are high-liability events.

QuestionText: The PRIMARY reason EMS providers must not use personal cell phones while driving the apparatus:
Choice1: It is illegal, dangerous, and a significant liability
Choice2: Signal is terrible in most response areas anyway
Choice3: Your thumbs need to be free for chest compressions
Choice4: Squad social media can wait until after patient contact
Feedback: Distracted driving is a leading cause of EMS vehicle collisions. Beyond the liability, you can't help the patient if you don't arrive.

QuestionText: After a 24-hour shift, the MOST important post-shift activity is:
Choice1: Rest, hydration, and self-care including a mental health check-in
Choice2: Replaying every call in detail instead of sleeping
Choice3: Immediately accepting overtime because the unit is short-staffed
Choice4: Telling your family all about it while they try to eat dinner
Feedback: Operational readiness starts with recovery. Fatigue degrades clinical judgment, reaction time, and communication. Taking care of yourself isn't optional — it's part of the job.
